mo, Spokane has not made the
most of this land rush." said an
official of the government today in
commenting on the manner in
which the visiting crowds were re-
ceived here during the registration
period now about to close. when
you consider the immense amount
of unoccupied government land,
that can be had on the homestead
basis without cost, and the vast
stretches of wheat and other cheap
lands for sale at nominal figures,
Spokane had great opportunity
to interest the land-hungry in this
region in something else besides
the Indian reservations.


But not much was done in this
direction by the commercial bodies
and but little by the real estate
men, who were too intent on pick
ing up stray quarters as registra-
ton notaries to try to show lands
to the visitors.


There Is a little town in North
Dakota where we once held land
opening that can give Spokane
some good pointers in profiting
from land rush. There every real
estate man and large land owner
got busy and showed the visitors
around and before the rush was
over had sold or located the new
corners on nearly as much land as
the reservation to be opened con-
tanned altogether.


Every acer of government land
in Idaho or Washington on which
a settler is located will help Spo-
kane, likewise all land sold for
farming purposes by private own
ers. Missoula and Occur d'Alene
have been much more active In
this respect than Spokane and have
waged a hot campaign for other
lands than those included In the
Indian reservations"
